content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance/Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660                322-5901            322-6441           322-6444


June 27, 1978


Robert E. Shaw
Mobil Oil Corporation
925 L Street, Suite 850
Sacramento, CA  95814

Dear Mr. Shaw:

     This will follow up on our meeting on Friday, June 23,
1978, and our June 26, 1978, telephone conversation.

     You are one of the lobbyists for Mobil Oil. You estab-
lished a lobbyist's bank account from which all expenses,
except rent and salary, were paid. You reported those pay-
ments on your lobbyist reports. Mobil's accounting depart-
ment in Dallas has proposed that this method be changed so
that all your expenses will be paid from Dallas. Check
requests for the expenses will be sent to Dallas either
from the Los Angeles office after that office approves the
request or from you directly. You have asked whether this
proposed method is permitted under the Political Reform Act
("Act").

     There is nothing in the Act which would prevent the use
of the proposed method. Your lobbyist reports will continue
to reflect all expenses incurred by you, even if Mobil pays
them. Since I assume you will be making the decisions
about where, whether or how expenses are made in connection
with your lobbying activities, you will be incurring them.
(See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18615(a)(6).) Therefore your
reports should not vary substantially from those reports you
filed under the previous method.

     Because you no longer will have a bank account which
records your lobbying receipts and expenditures, it will
be necessary for you to establish a designated ledger
account in which lobbying receipts and expenditures are
reported. Activity expenses must be disclosed in the lobby-
ist report covering the period in which the expense is

Robert E. Shaw                                June 27, 1978
Page Two


incurred, regardless of when it is paid.  2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18615(b)(1)(A).  Your general lobbying expenses may,
but need not, be accrued.  2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18615
(b)(1)(B).

     Since Mobil Oil will be paying the expenses incurred by
you, it must report, as a lump sum, the amount of those
payments in assistance of a lobbyist on page 2 of its lobby-
ist employer report.  The lobbyist employer is not required
to report the itemization of expenses which appear on the
lobbyist's report.  See Opinion requested by John Witt, 1
FPPC Opinions 145, 146 (No. 75-057, Oct. 23, 1975.)

     You specifically inquired about the cash position summary
on page 3 of the lobbyist report.  That portion is to be
completed only by lobbyists who have designated a bank
account as their lobbyist account.  Therefore you will no
longer be filling out that summary.  Since your previous
reports did have that portion completed, you might want to
attach an explanation to your first report using the new
method noting that the cash position summary portion is no
longer applicable.

     I hope this information is helpful.  If you have any
questions, please contact me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Barbara Campbell
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

sections.question: s, please contact me.
sections.conclusion: There is nothing in the Act which would prevent the use of the proposed method. Lobbyist reports will continue to reflect all expenses incurred by the lobbyist. The lobbyist must establish a designated ledger account to record receipts and expenditures instead of maintaining a bank account. Activity expenses must be disclosed when incurred, not when paid. Mobil Oil must report payments as a lump sum in assistance of a lobbyist on page 2 of its employer report. The lobbyist is no longer required to complete the cash position summary section of the report.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether Mobil Oil's proposed change to pay all lobbying expenses directly from Dallas, rather than through a designated lobbyist bank account, complies with the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The proposed payment method is permissible under the Political Reform Act, provided the lobbyist maintains a designated ledger account instead of a bank account and continues reporting all expenses when incurred, while Mobil Oil reports payments as a lump sum on its employer report.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document is a follow-up letter after in-person meeting and telephone conversation. Clear question presented informally within letter text. Contains specific regulatory citations and detailed compliance guidance.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: s, please contact me.

CONCLUSION: There is nothing in the Act which would prevent the use of the proposed method. Lobbyist reports will continue to reflect all expenses incurred by the lobbyist. The lobbyist must establish a designated ledger account to record receipts and expenditures instead of maintaining a bank account. Activity expenses must be disclosed when incurred, not when paid. Mobil Oil must report payments as a lump sum in assistance of a lobbyist on page 2 of its employer report. The lobbyist is no longer required to complete the cash position summary section of the report.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises Robert Shaw, a lobbyist for Mobil Oil, that Mobil's proposed method of paying all lobbying expenses directly from Dallas is permitted under the Political Reform Act, with specified reporting requirements for both the lobbyist and Mobil Oil.
